name: Thompson’s or Simmond’s Test
purpose: To assess for Achilles tendon rupture.
instructions: Patient lies prone with feet hanging off the edge of the table.
Examiner squeezes the calf muscle.
Positive: Absence of plantar flexion indicates Achilles tendon rupture.

name: Talar Tilt Test
purpose: To assess the integrity of the calcaneofibular ligament (CFL).
instructions: Patient seated with legs hanging.
Examiner stabilizes the distal tibia and inverts the calcaneus.
Positive: Excessive laxity or pain relative to the unaffected side.

name: Anterior Drawer Test (Ankle)
purpose: To assess the integrity of the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL).
instructions: Patient supine with foot in 20° plantar flexion.
Examiner stabilizes the tibia and pulls the calcaneus anteriorly.
Positive: Excessive anterior translation of the talus.

name: Squeeze Test
purpose: To assess for syndesmosis (high ankle) injury.
instructions: Patient seated or supine.
Examiner squeezes the tibia and fibula together at mid-calf level.
Positive: Pain elicited at the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis.

name: Tinel’s Sign
purpose: To detect irritated nerves by percussing over the nerve.
instructions: Examiner taps over the suspected nerve pathway (e.g., carpal tunnel or tarsal tunnel).
Positive: Tingling or 'pins and needles' sensation in the nerve distribution.

name: Dorsiflexion–Eversion Test
purpose: To assess for Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome.
instructions: Examiner passively dorsiflexes and everts the foot to its end-range.
Hold for 5-10 seconds while percussing over the tarsal tunnel.
Positive: Reproduction of symptoms along the plantar aspect of the foot.

name: Spurling’s Test
purpose: To assess for cervical radiculopathy.
instructions: Patient's head is side-flexed to the affected side.
Examiner applies a downward axial load to the head.
Positive: Pain radiating into the arm on the same side.

name: Cervical Distraction Test
purpose: To assess for cervical radiculopathy (relief test).
instructions: Patient supine.
Examiner applies upward traction to the patient's head.
Positive: Reduction or elimination of radicular symptoms.

name: Maudsley’s Test
purpose: To assess for lateral epicondylalgia (Tennis Elbow) involving the extensor digitorum.
instructions: Patient's elbow is extended.
Examiner resists extension of the third digit (middle finger).
Positive: Pain over the lateral epicondyle.

name: Cozen’s Test
purpose: To assess for lateral epicondylalgia.
instructions: Patient's elbow is flexed to 90°, forearm pronated.
Examiner resists active wrist extension and radial deviation.
Positive: Sudden pain at the lateral epicondyle.

name: Mill’s Sign
purpose: To assess for lateral epicondylalgia.
instructions: Examiner passively pronates the forearm, flexes the wrist fully, and extends the elbow.
Positive: Pain over the lateral epicondyle.

name: Valgus Stress Test
purpose: To assess the integrity of the Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL).
instructions: Patient supine, knee in 20-30° flexion.
Examiner applies a valgus (inward) force to the knee while stabilizing the ankle.
Positive: Excessive medial opening or pain.

name: O’Driscoll’s Hook Test
purpose: To assess for distal biceps tendon rupture.
instructions: Patient's elbow is flexed to 90°, forearm supinated.
Examiner attempts to hook the lateral edge of the biceps tendon with their finger.
Positive: Inability to hook the tendon.

name: Straight-Leg Raise (Lasegue’s Test)
purpose: To assess for lumbar nerve root irritation.
instructions: Patient supine.
Examiner slowly lifts the symptomatic leg with the knee extended.
Positive: Radicular pain below the knee between 30-70°.

name: Bragard Test
purpose: Confirmatory test for neural origin in SLR.
instructions: Perform SLR to the level of pain, then lower the leg 5°.
Passively dorsiflex the foot.
Positive: Reproduction of radicular symptoms.

name: Bowstring Test
purpose: To assess for sciatic nerve tension.
instructions: Perform SLR to paint point, then flex the knee slightly (20°).
Apply pressure to the popliteal nerve in the fossa.
Positive: Reproduction of radicular symptoms.

name: Bicycle Stress Test
purpose: To differentiate between neurogenic and vascular claudication.
instructions: Patient pedals a stationary bike in an upright posture until symptoms appear.
Patient pedals again while leaning forward (flexed spine).
Positive for neurogenic: Increased exercise tolerance when flexed.

name: Two-Stage Treadmill Test
purpose: To assess for spinal stenosis.
instructions: Test 1: Patient walks on a level treadmill.
Test 2: Patient walks on an uphill treadmill.
Positive: Symptoms take longer to appear or are less severe on the uphill (flexed) walk.

name: Lumbar Extension-Loading Test
purpose: To assess for facet joint syndrome or spondylolysis.
instructions: Patient stands and extends the lumbar spine while rotating toward the symptomatic side.
Positive: Reproduction of localized low back pain.

name: Femoral Nerve Stretch Test (Wasserman Sign)
purpose: To assess for L2-L4 nerve root irritation.
instructions: Patient prone.
Examiner flexes the patient's knee and then extends the hip.
Positive: Pain in the anterior thigh.

name: Kernig Test
purpose: To assess for meningeal irritation or dural tension.
instructions: Patient supine with hip and knee flexed to 90°.
Examiner attempts to passively extend the knee.
Positive: Resistance to extension and pain in the neck or back.

name: Milgram Test
purpose: To assess for increased intrathecal pressure (e.g., disc herniation).
instructions: Patient supine.
Patient is asked to lift both legs 2 inches off the table and hold for 30 seconds.
Positive: Inability to hold or reproduction of symptoms.

name: Lateral Lumbar Flexion (Finger Tip to Floor Test)
purpose: To assess lumbar mobility and asymmetry.
instructions: Patient stands with feet together.
Patient slides their hand down the side of their leg as far as possible.
Measure distance from third finger to floor.
Positive: Significant asymmetry between sides.

name: Modified Schober’s Test
purpose: To measure lumbar spine flexion range of motion.
instructions: Mark S2 spinous process and 10cm above, 5cm below.
Patient bends forward as far as possible.
Positive: Extension of distance less than 5cm suggests restricted mobility (e.g., AS).

name: Painful Arc Test
purpose: To assess for subacromial impingement.
instructions: Patient actively abducts the arm in the scapular plane.
Positive: Pain between 60° and 120° of abduction.

name: Neer Impingement Sign
purpose: To assess for subacromial impingement.
instructions: Examiner stabilizes the scapula and passively flexes the arm to its end-range.
Positive: Pain in the subacromial region.

name: Hawkins–Kennedy Test
purpose: To assess for subacromial impingement.
instructions: Patient's shoulder and elbow flexed to 90°.
Examiner forces internal rotation of the humerus.
Positive: Pain in the subacromial region.

name: Jobe’s Test
purpose: To assess the supraspinatus tendon.
instructions: Arm at 90° abduction, 30° horizontal flexion, full internal rotation ('thumb down').
Examiner applies downward pressure while patient resists.
Positive: Pain or weakness.

name: Empty Can Test (Supraspinatus)
purpose: Identical to Jobe's test, specifically for supraspinatus.
instructions: Abduct arm to 90°, 30° forward, internal rotation.
Resist downward force.
Positive: Weakness or pain.

name: Infraspinatus Test
purpose: To assess the infraspinatus tendon.
instructions: Arm at side, elbow flexed to 90°.
Examiner resists the patient’s attempt at external rotation.
Positive: Pain or weakness.

name: Subscapularis (Belly Press Test)
purpose: To assess the subscapularis muscle when internal rotation is limited.
instructions: Patient places hand on belly and presses inward.
Positive: Elbow drops behind the plane of the body or inability to press.

name: Lag Sign (External Rotation)
purpose: To assess for full-thickness infraspinatus/supraspinatus tears.
instructions: Arm at side, flexed elbow, examiner passively rotates arm to full external rotation.
Patient is asked to hold the position.
Positive: 'Lag' or inability to maintain the position.

name: Drop Sign
purpose: To assess for infraspinatus tears.
instructions: Arm in 90° abduction, elbow flexed 90°, full external rotation.
Examiner releases the arm and patient tries to hold.
Positive: Arm 'drops' toward internal rotation.

name: Lift-Off Test
purpose: To assess the subscapularis muscle.
instructions: Patient places the back of their hand on their lumbar spine.
Patient is asked to lift the hand away from the back.
Positive: Inability to lift the hand.

name: Speed Test
purpose: To assess for bicipital tendinopathy.
instructions: Patient's arm extended, forearm supinated.
Examiner resists active shoulder flexion from 0 to 60°.
Positive: Pain in the bicipital groove.

name: Yergason’s Test
purpose: To assess for bicipital tendinopathy or SLAP lesions.
instructions: Elbow flexed to 90°, forearm pronated.
Examiner resists the patient’s attempt to supinate and externally rotate.
Positive: Pain in the bicipital groove.

name: O’Brien’s Test
purpose: To assess for SLAP lesions or AC joint pathology.
instructions: Part 1: Arm 90° flexion, 10° adduction, full internal rotation. Resist downward force.
Part 2: Repeat with full external rotation.
Positive: Pain during Part 1 that is relieved during Part 2.

name: Apprehension Test
purpose: To assess for anterior shoulder instability.
instructions: Arm 90° abduction, 90° external rotation.
Examiner slowly increases external rotation.
Positive: Patient looks apprehensive or expresses fear of dislocation.

name: Fulcrum’s Test
purpose: To assess for anterior shoulder instability.
instructions: Perform Apprehension Test while placing a hand under the glenohumeral joint (fulcrum).
Positive: Reproduction of symptoms or apprehension.

name: Drawer’s Test (Knee)
purpose: To assess the integrity of the ACL or PCL.
instructions: Patient supine, knee flexed to 90°.
Examiner pulls the tibia anteriorly (ACL) or pushes posteriorly (PCL).
Positive: Excessive translation of the tibia.

name: FABER Test (Patrick's Test)
purpose: To detect intra-articular hip pathology such as osteoarthritis or FAI, and to screen the SIJ.
instructions: Patient supine; place the affected leg in the figure-4 position (flexion, abduction, external rotation).
Apply gentle downward pressure on the flexed knee while stabilizing the opposite ASIS.
Positive: Pain reproduced in the groin or restricted movement compared to the other side.

name: FADIR Test (Flexion, Adduction, Internal Rotation)
purpose: To detect femoroacetabular impingement and intra-articular/labral pathology.
instructions: Patient supine; flex the hip to 90 degrees.
Adduct and internally rotate the hip.
Positive: Groin pain (or clicking) reproduced.

name: Scour Test (Hip Quadrant)
purpose: To assess hip joint degeneration and intra-articular pathology (OA, FAI, labral).
instructions: Patient supine; flex and adduct the hip.
Apply axial compression through the femur while moving the hip in an arc.
Positive: Pain, grinding, clicking, or crepitus felt.

name: Log Roll Test
purpose: To detect intra-articular hip pathology (the most specific test for the hip joint).
instructions: Patient supine with legs extended and relaxed.
Gently roll the leg internally and externally.
Positive: Pain reproduced, or increased external rotation compared to the other side.

name: Trendelenburg Test
purpose: To assess gluteus medius strength and detect gluteal weakness (OA, GTPS).
instructions: Patient stands on the affected leg only; examiner observes the pelvis from behind.
Positive: The pelvis drops on the opposite (contralateral) side.

name: Single-Leg Stance Test (Hip)
purpose: Highly sensitive screen for gluteal tendinopathy / GTPS.
instructions: Patient stands on the affected leg for up to 30 seconds.
Positive: Lateral hip pain reproduced within 30 seconds.

name: Resisted External Derotation Test
purpose: To detect gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy (GTPS).
instructions: Patient supine, hip flexed to 90 degrees; examiner passively externally rotates the hip.
Ask the patient to return the leg to neutral against resistance.
Positive: Pain reproduced over the lateral hip.

name: Ober's Test
purpose: To detect iliotibial band tightness contributing to GTPS or lateral snapping hip.
instructions: Patient side-lying with the unaffected leg down.
Extend and abduct the affected hip, then allow it to drop into adduction.
Positive: The leg remains abducted and does not drop.

name: Dynamic Hip Movement Test
purpose: To confirm snapping hip syndrome.
instructions: Patient actively flexes and extends the hip while the examiner palpates over the anterior (iliopsoas) or lateral (ITB) hip.
Positive: Snapping is reproduced (visible, palpable, or audible).

name: Thomas Test (Modified, Hip)
purpose: To detect hip flexor tightness, iliopsoas involvement, or labral irritation.
instructions: Patient supine at the edge of the table; pull one knee to the chest while the other leg hangs off the table.
Positive: The hanging leg produces hip pain, clicking, or fails to extend fully.

name: Internal Rotation Overpressure Test
purpose: To detect FAI due to limited internal rotation.
instructions: Patient supine, hip flexed to 90 degrees; apply internal rotation with overpressure.
Positive: Groin pain or restriction noted.

name: Phalen's Test
purpose: To provoke median nerve symptoms in carpal tunnel syndrome.
instructions: Patient fully flexes both wrists and holds the backs of the hands together.
Hold the position for up to 60 seconds.
Positive: Numbness or tingling in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial ring).

name: Tinel's Sign (Wrist)
purpose: To detect median nerve irritation at the carpal tunnel.
instructions: Examiner taps lightly over the carpal tunnel on the palmar wrist.
Positive: Tingling or 'pins and needles' in the median nerve distribution.

name: Carpal Compression Test (Durkan's)
purpose: To reproduce CTS symptoms via direct carpal tunnel pressure.
instructions: Examiner applies firm, direct pressure over the carpal tunnel with both thumbs for 30 seconds.
Positive: Reproduction of numbness/tingling in the median nerve distribution.

name: Finkelstein's Test
purpose: To detect De Quervain's tenosynovitis.
instructions: Patient makes a fist with the thumb tucked inside the fingers.
Examiner passively ulnar-deviates the wrist.
Positive: Sharp pain over the radial styloid (thumb side of the wrist).

name: WHAT Test (Wrist Hyperflexion & Abduction of Thumb)
purpose: To detect De Quervain's tenosynovitis.
instructions: Patient hyperflexes the wrist and abducts the thumb against examiner resistance.
Positive: Pain reproduced over the radial wrist.

name: Wrist Ligament Stress Tests
purpose: To detect ligamentous injury (wrist sprain).
instructions: Examiner applies directed stress to specific wrist ligaments (e.g., scapholunate, lunotriquetral).
Positive: Pain or laxity at the stressed ligament.

name: TFCC Load Test (Ulnar Grind)
purpose: To detect triangular fibrocartilage complex injury.
instructions: Hold the wrist in ulnar deviation, apply axial load, and rotate the forearm.
Positive: Pain or clicking on the ulnar side of the wrist.

name: Press Test
purpose: Highly sensitive functional screen for a TFCC tear.
instructions: Patient pushes up from a chair using the affected hand to bear weight.
Positive: Ulnar-sided wrist pain reproduced.

name: Transillumination Test (Wrist)
purpose: To confirm that a wrist mass is fluid-filled (ganglion cyst).
instructions: Apply a light source through the swelling in a darkened room.
Positive: Light passes through, indicating a fluid-filled cyst rather than a solid mass.

name: Resisted Wrist Extension Test
purpose: To detect intersection syndrome.
instructions: Patient extends the wrist against examiner resistance.
Positive: Pain or crepitus over the dorsal radial forearm (4-6 cm proximal to the wrist).

name: Anatomical Snuffbox Tenderness Test
purpose: Most important clinical sign of a scaphoid fracture.
instructions: Palpate the anatomical snuffbox at the base of the thumb.
Positive: Localized tenderness — treat as scaphoid fracture until imaging excludes it.

name: Scaphoid Compression Test
purpose: To support suspicion of a scaphoid fracture.
instructions: Apply axial load (compression) along the line of the thumb metacarpal toward the scaphoid.
Positive: Pain reproduced in the scaphoid region.

name: Axial Load Test (Wrist / Lunate)
purpose: Non-specific provocation suggesting intra-articular pathology (e.g., Kienbock's). No single definitive test exists.
instructions: Apply axial compression through the wrist.
Positive: Central dorsal wrist pain — correlate with imaging; Kienbock's is imaging-dependent.

name: Clarke's Test (Patellar Grind)
purpose: To detect patellofemoral joint dysfunction (PFPS).
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Apply gentle downward pressure on the superior pole of the patella.
Ask the patient to contract the quadriceps.
Positive: Anterior knee pain reproduced.

name: Step-Down Test
purpose: To detect PFPS and poor lower-limb control.
instructions: Patient stands on a 20 cm step and slowly lowers the opposite heel to the ground.
Observe knee alignment.
Positive: Anterior knee pain or medial collapse (dynamic valgus).

name: Patellar Tilt Test
purpose: To detect tight lateral structures contributing to PFPS / OA.
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Lift the lateral edge of the patella.
Positive: The lateral edge cannot reach the horizontal.

name: Waldron Test
purpose: To detect patellofemoral joint irritation (PFPS).
instructions: Patient actively flexes and extends the knee while the examiner palpates the patellofemoral joint.
Positive: Grinding, crepitus, or anterior knee pain.

name: Patellar Glide Test
purpose: To detect patellar maltracking (PFPS).
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Glide the patella medially and laterally.
Positive: Pain or abnormal patellar mobility.

name: McMurray Test
purpose: To detect a meniscal tear.
instructions: Patient supine. Fully flex the hip and knee, then rotate the tibia and extend the knee.
Positive: Palpable click/snap or joint-line pain (internal rotation = lateral meniscus; external = medial).

name: Apley's Compression Test
purpose: To detect a meniscal injury.
instructions: Patient prone, knee flexed 90 degrees. Apply downward compression while rotating the tibia.
Positive: Joint-line pain on compression.

name: Thessaly Test
purpose: To detect a meniscal tear (weight-bearing).
instructions: Patient stands on one leg, knee flexed ~20 degrees, and rotates the body/knee three times.
Positive: Joint-line pain or a sense of locking/catching.

name: Joint Line Tenderness
purpose: To detect meniscal or tibiofemoral pathology.
instructions: Palpate along the medial and lateral joint lines.
Positive: Localized joint-line tenderness.

name: Bounce Home Test
purpose: To detect a displaced meniscal fragment (e.g., bucket-handle tear).
instructions: Patient supine. Fully flex the knee, then allow it to passively extend.
Positive: Knee does not fully extend (block) or has a springy end-feel.

name: Resisted Contraction Test
purpose: To detect a muscle strain.
instructions: Patient contracts the affected muscle against resistance at mid-range.
Positive: Pain at the muscle site.

name: Passive Stretch Test
purpose: To detect a muscle strain.
instructions: Examiner slowly stretches the affected muscle.
Positive: Pain at the muscle site.

name: Lachman Test
purpose: To detect an ACL tear (most sensitive ACL test).
instructions: Knee flexed ~30 degrees. Stabilize the femur and pull the tibia anteriorly.
Positive: Soft endpoint or increased anterior tibial translation.

name: Anterior Drawer Test (Knee)
purpose: To detect an ACL injury.
instructions: Knee flexed 90 degrees, foot stabilized. Pull the tibia anteriorly.
Positive: Increased anterior translation versus the other side.

name: Varus Stress Test
purpose: To detect an LCL injury.
instructions: Knee flexed 30 degrees. Stabilize the femur and apply a varus (outward) force.
Positive: Pain or gapping at the lateral joint line (Grade I/II/III by laxity).

name: Posterior Drawer Test
purpose: To detect a PCL tear.
instructions: Knee flexed 90 degrees. Push the tibia posteriorly.
Positive: Increased posterior translation.

name: Godfrey (Posterior Sag) Test
purpose: To detect a PCL injury.
instructions: Hip and knee flexed 90 degrees, examiner supports the leg. Observe the tibial plateau.
Positive: The tibia sags posteriorly versus the other side.

name: Patellofemoral Compression Test (Grind)
purpose: To detect patellofemoral OA.
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Apply downward pressure on the patella while the patient contracts the quadriceps.
Positive: Pain or crepitus at the patellofemoral joint.

name: Crepitus Test
purpose: To detect articular cartilage degeneration (OA).
instructions: Move the knee through full flexion and extension while palpating.
Positive: Audible or palpable crepitus/grinding.

name: Range of Motion Assessment (Knee)
purpose: To detect joint degeneration (OA).
instructions: Passively flex and extend the knee, noting pain, stiffness, and limited ROM.
Positive: Reduced flexion or extension.

name: Patellar Apprehension Test
purpose: To detect lateral patellar instability / prior dislocation.
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended and relaxed. Gently push the patella laterally.
Positive: Apprehension or quadriceps contraction to resist displacement.

name: Patella Tracking Assessment
purpose: To detect abnormal patellar motion / prior dislocation.
instructions: Observe the patella during active knee flexion and extension.
Positive: The patella deviates laterally or fails to track smoothly.

name: Patellar Hypermobility Test
purpose: To detect generalized patellar laxity.
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Displace the patella medially and laterally.
Positive: Excessive movement (>50% of patellar width).

name: Q-Angle Measurement
purpose: To detect malalignment predisposing to lateral patellar dislocation.
instructions: Measure the angle between the ASIS-patella line and the patella-tibial tuberosity line with the knee extended.
Positive: Q-angle >15 degrees (men) or >20 degrees (women).

name: Straight Leg Raise Test (Knee)
purpose: To detect extensor mechanism disruption / patella fracture.
instructions: Patient supine, legs extended. Ask the patient to lift the affected leg straight without bending the knee.
Positive: Inability to actively lift the leg.

name: Patellar Integrity Palpation
purpose: To detect a patella fracture.
instructions: Palpate the patella gently for tenderness, irregularity, or a gap.
Positive: Severe localized tenderness, palpable gap, or irregularity.

name: Active Knee Extension Test
purpose: To detect a patella fracture / extensor disruption.
instructions: Ask the patient to actively straighten the knee from a flexed position.
Positive: Inability or severe pain during active extension.

name: Axial Loading Test (Tibia)
purpose: To detect a tibial plateau fracture.
instructions: Apply gentle axial compression through the heel toward the tibia.
Positive: Severe pain at the tibial plateau region.

name: Noble Compression Test
purpose: To detect iliotibial band syndrome.
instructions: Patient supine, knee flexed 90 degrees. Apply pressure over the lateral femoral epicondyle while slowly extending the knee.
Positive: Pain at approximately 30 degrees of knee flexion.

name: Ober's Test (Knee / ITBS)
purpose: To detect iliotibial band tightness contributing to ITBS.
instructions: Patient side-lying on the unaffected side. Extend and abduct the hip, then slowly lower the leg.
Positive: The leg remains elevated and does not drop.

name: Renne Test
purpose: To detect iliotibial band syndrome (weight-bearing).
instructions: Patient stands and performs a squat while the examiner presses over the lateral femoral epicondyle.
Positive: Pain at approximately 30 degrees of knee flexion.

name: Decline Squat Test
purpose: To detect patellar tendinopathy.
instructions: Patient stands on a 25-degree decline board and performs a squat.
Positive: Localized pain at the inferior pole of the patella.

name: Resisted Knee Extension Test
purpose: To detect patellar/quadriceps tendinopathy.
instructions: Patient seated, knee flexed. Resist knee extension.
Positive: Localized tendon pain.

name: Single-Leg Decline Squat Test
purpose: To detect patellar tendinopathy.
instructions: Patient performs a single-leg squat on a decline surface.
Positive: Reproduction of localized tendon pain.

name: Hop Test (Knee)
purpose: Functional indicator of patellar/quadriceps tendinopathy.
instructions: Patient performs repeated hopping on the affected leg.
Positive: Pain localized to the patellar or quadriceps tendon.

name: Pes Anserine Palpation Test
purpose: To detect pes anserine bursitis.
instructions: Patient supine, knee extended. Palpate 5-7 cm below the medial joint line.
Positive: Localized tenderness (below, not at, the joint line).

name: Resisted Knee Flexion Test
purpose: To detect pes anserine bursitis.
instructions: Patient flexes the knee against resistance.
Positive: Pain at the pes anserine region.

name: Resisted Hip Adduction Test
purpose: To detect pes anserine bursitis (sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus).
instructions: Patient performs hip adduction against resistance.
Positive: Pain at the pes anserine insertion.

name: Wilson's Test
purpose: To detect osteochondritis dissecans of the medial femoral condyle.
instructions: Patient supine, knee flexed 90 degrees. Patient actively extends the knee while the examiner internally rotates the tibia.
Positive: Pain at 30-40 degrees relieved by external rotation of the tibia.

name: Passive Condylar Palpation Test
purpose: To detect an OCD lesion.
instructions: Knee flexed 90 degrees. Palpate the femoral condyles.
Positive: Localized pain over a femoral condyle.

name: Joint Effusion (Stroke) Test
purpose: To detect joint effusion associated with intra-articular pathology.
instructions: Stroke the medial knee upward, then the lateral knee downward.
Positive: A fluid wave or bulge appears.

name: Foucher's Sign
purpose: To confirm a Baker's cyst.
instructions: Palpate the popliteal mass with the knee extended, then flexed.
Positive: The mass is firm in extension and softens/disappears in flexion.

name: Popliteal Palpation Test
purpose: To detect a Baker's cyst.
instructions: Patient prone or supine. Palpate the popliteal fossa.
Positive: A palpable fluid-filled mass.

name: Knee Effusion Test (Bulge / Stroke)
purpose: To detect intra-articular effusion.
instructions: Perform the bulge or stroke test over the knee.
Positive: Fluid accumulation present.

name: Passive Joint Motion Test (Knee)
purpose: To screen for septic arthritis. AVOID aggressive testing.
instructions: Gently move the knee through a small range of passive flexion/extension.
Positive: Severe pain even with minimal passive movement.

name: Temperature Assessment (Knee)
purpose: To detect joint infection.
instructions: Compare the temperature of the affected and unaffected knee with the back of the hand.
Positive: The affected knee is significantly warmer.

name: Slump Test
purpose: To assess for lumbar nerve root irritation / neural tension (dural mobility).
instructions: Patient sits upright on the edge of the couch with hands behind the back.
Patient slumps the spine into thoracic and lumbar flexion; examiner overpresses the shoulders.
Patient flexes the neck (chin to chest); examiner maintains overpressure.
Examiner extends the symptomatic knee, then dorsiflexes the ankle.
Positive: reproduction of the patient’s radicular leg pain, relieved by releasing neck flexion.
